title: Former Clemson center Christian Reeves is back in the transfer portal
description: Former Clemson and Duke big man Christian Reeves is back in the transfer portal after a strong year at College of Charleston.
fullText:
Another former Clemson basketball player is on the move again.

Christian Reeves is back in the transfer portal after a productive season at College of Charleston. The 7-foot-2 center made a strong impact, averaging 11.1 points, 7.8 rebounds and 1.4 blocks per game. His performance earned him third-team All-CAA honors and helped raise his profile heading into the offseason.

Reeves began his college career at Duke before transferring to Clemson for the 2024-25 season. His time with the Tigers never fully developed, as injuries limited him to just 145 minutes across 29 appearances. He finished that year averaging 1.2 points and 1.5 rebounds, struggling to carve out a consistent role.

After showing clear improvement at Charleston, Reeves will now look for another opportunity.

Clemson, meanwhile, is coming off a season that ended with a loss to Iowa in the 2026 NCAA Tournament, leaving the program looking to reload heading into next year.

title: Oklahoma State guard Jaylen Curry plans to enter NCAA transfer portal
description: Oklahoma State guard Jaylen Curry plans to enter the NCAA transfer portal, On3’s Joe Tipton has learned. Curry is on the move for the second consecutive offseason, only spending one year in Stillwater. UMass is where his college basketball career got underway. But now, Curry is looking for school No. 3 for what should be […]
fullText:
Oklahoma State guard Jaylen Curry plans to enter the NCAA transfer portal, On3’s Joe Tipton has learned. Curry is on the move for the second consecutive offseason, only spending one year in Stillwater. UMass is where his college basketball career got underway. But now, Curry is looking for school No. 3 for what should be his final year of eligibility.

The Cowboys used Curry as a nice rotational piece throughout his 33 games played. Only seven of them were starts, playing around 24 minutes every time out there. Curry averaged 10.1 points while shooting 41.8% from the field. Not too bad for somebody playing in a power conference for the first time in his career.

Curry did a little more than just score, though. He finished as Oklahoma State’s second-leading assist man, putting up 3.5 per game. Some activity on the glass did some — mainly on the defensive end — with 3.2 rebounds a night.

To keep up with the latest players on the move, check out On3’s Transfer Portal wire. The On3 Transfer Portal Instagram account and Twitter account are excellent resources to stay up to date with the latest moves.

There is one performance from this past season that stands out more than any. Oklahoma State hosted Nicholls back in November for a nonconference showdown. Curry had his lone double-double of the year, doing so in style. Thirty points were put up by Curry, going 8-11 from the field and making all 12 of his free throws. And then 10 assists also show up on the statsheet to help Oklahoma State get a 14-point win.

Curry played high school basketball at Charlotte (NC) Calvary Christian, where he was a three-star prospect. He was the No. 193 overall recruit in the 2023 cycle, according to the Rivals Industry Ranking, a weighted average that utilizes all four major recruiting media companies.

UMass earned Curry’s original commitment and let him get action as a true freshman. One season later, Curry was a starter for the Minutemen and putting up good numbers. During the 2024-25 season, he averaged 13.3 points, 3.8 rebounds, and 3.9 assists. Unfortunately, not too much team success came, and Curry decided to enter the transfer portal.

Now, another school will have an opportunity to sign Curry. If the fit turns out to be the right one, some potential still appears to be in there.

title: Nearly Half of College Students Are Seriously Rethinking Their Plans as AI Concerns Grow
description: Close to half of undergrads have given serious thought to changing plans because of concerns about AI's impact on jobs, according to a survey from Lumina Foundation and Gallup.
fullText:
About half of undergrads say they've given serious thought to changing plans because of concerns about AI's impact on jobs, according to a recent survey.

Students in vocational and technology programs were the most likely to report they made a switch because of AI.

Worries about AI's impact on the labor market are leading many college students to consider a change of plans.

Almost half (47%) of undergrads said they've given at least a "fair amount" of thought to changing their field of study because of concerns about AI-driven disruption, according to a study from Lumina Foundation and Gallup released last week.

A tough job market for recent grads and a spate of high-profile layoffs thanks in part to AI have raised anxieties about the value of postsecondary degrees and what to study.

Most of the roughly 4,000 associate and bachelor’s degree students surveyed, though, said they haven’t made changes—yet.

Just 16% of students surveyed said they've already switched their field of study because of AI. Of those, students in vocational programs were the most likely to say AI influenced their choice, followed by those studying technology.

That tracks with other studies showing a surge of interest in vocational programs. Hands-on jobs requiring in-person work are expected to be among those least likely to be automated by AI.

If you want to land a high-paying job and capitalize on the demand for AI while in a traditional four-year program, majors in technology that develop math and coding skills could be among the safest bets.

However, majors like computer science are now among those with the highest unemployment rates for recent grads, according to data from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York.

If you're a student debating which major to choose, comparing majors by employer demand and salaries can help you get the best return on your return on your investment.

title: North Carolina ends head coach search with splash for NBA champion
description: The North Carolina Tar Heels stunned college basketball before the 2026 March Madness title game by hiring an NBA champion to coach next season. The ACC powerhouse suffered a brutal Round of 64 loss against VCU in this year’s NCAA…
fullText:
The North Carolina Tar Heels stunned college basketball before the 2026 March Madness title game by hiring an NBA champion to coach next season.

The ACC powerhouse suffered a brutal Round of 64 loss against VCU in this year’s NCAA Tournament. However, despite links to other current college coaches the Tar Heels are turning to the NBA to rebuild the program.

MORE: NCAA schools with both men’s and women’s basketball titles

ESPN insider Pete Thamel revealed Monday that North Carolina plans on hiring former Denver Nuggets head coach Michael Malone. The former NBA champion coach helped guide the Nuggets to an NBA championship in 2023.

Sources: North Carolina intends to hire longtime NBA coach Michael Malone as the school’s next basketball coach. He’s an NBA Championship coach with the Denver Nuggets from the 2022-23 season and has won 510 games as an NBA head coach. pic.twitter.com/JwJmUNRLQT

— Pete Thamel (@PeteThamel) April 6, 2026

Despite his NBA success, Malone was fired by Denver prior to the 2025 NBA Playoffs. The Western Conference franchise shocked many fans when the move was made days before the postseason tipped off.

However, Malone’s final six full seasons in Denver resulted in a title, conference finals appearance and two trips to the West semifinals. The 54 year old recently shifted into broadcasting with ESPN after his 10-year stint with Denver.

North Carolina hopes to bring stability back to its program after Hubert Davis’ recent firing. Tar Heels faithful were extremely critical over Davis’ coaching style and lack of success come postseason time.

MORE: Michigan, UConn battle for 2026 national championship crown

UNC is a consistent powerhouse in the college basketball landscape. However, Roy Williams’ retirement from the school in 2021 led Davis taking charge over the last five seasons.

The Tar Heels managed to reach one Final Four and a Sweet Sixteen under Davis, but missed the NCAA Tournament entirely in 2023 before posting first-round exits in 2025 and 2026.

The move for Malone is slightly surprising from a North Carolina perspective considering his lack of ties to the university. However, Malone’s ability to work with top NBA stars in the past is likely very appealing to Tar Heels brass.

The growing influence of NIL and the transfer portal is very similar to how professional leagues like the NBA operate. Perhaps North Carolina wants a higher-level coach to help cultivate a more NBA-like atmosphere at one of the premier basketball schools on the East Coast.

title: Top college baseball freshmen to watch in 2026
description: Every college baseball season introduces a new wave of talent, but the 2026 freshman class feels particularly loaded with immediate-impact players and long-term stars. Across the country, from powerhouse SEC programs to rising contenders on the West Coast, first-year players…
fullText:
Every college baseball season introduces a new wave of talent, but the 2026 freshman class feels particularly loaded with immediate-impact players and long-term stars. Across the country, from powerhouse SEC programs to rising contenders on the West Coast, first-year players are not just filling roster spots—they are stepping directly into meaningful roles and reshaping expectations. This group blends advanced hitters with polished approaches, power arms with professional-level projection, and versatile athletes capable of contributing in multiple phases of the game.

What stands out most is how prepared many of these freshmen already look against high-level collegiate competition. Hitters like Angel Laya and Teddy Tokheim have demonstrated an ability to control at-bats and drive the baseball with authority, while players such as Jacob Parker and Nicholas Partida bring a level of completeness rarely seen this early in a college career. On the mound, arms like Cam Appenzeller and Sam Cozart showcase the kind of velocity and physicality typically associated with upperclassmen, hinting at future dominance as they refine their command.

Equally compelling is the depth of the class. Beyond the headline names, players like Michael Senay, Rett Johnson, and Caden Glauber offer diverse skill sets and high ceilings, suggesting that breakout performances could emerge throughout the season. Collectively, this freshman group reflects a broader trend in college baseball — young players arriving more polished, more confident, and more ready than ever before. As the 2026 season unfolds, these newcomers are poised not only to contribute but to define the trajectory of their programs.

Angel Laya has quickly emerged as one of the most polished freshman hitters in the country, already producing at an elite level in a major conference. His combination of physicality and barrel control allows him to impact the baseball with authority, reflected in his strong early slash line and power output. Evaluators have noted that he plays with the poise of a veteran, rarely looking overmatched against advanced pitching. Laya’s early inclusion on midseason All-American teams underscores how rapidly he has transitioned from prospect to centerpiece.

Teddy Tokheim has been one of the most explosive freshman bats in college baseball, blending plate discipline with high-end power production. His ability to drive the ball to all fields while maintaining a disciplined approach has made him a constant offensive threat in Stanford’s lineup. What separates Tokheim is not just production, but efficiency—he consistently turns quality at-bats into tangible results. His early statistical dominance places him among the most impactful freshmen in the nation.

Jacob Parker arrived on campus with significant pedigree and has validated that reputation with a steady, high-impact freshman campaign. Ranked among the top incoming players nationally, Parker has shown the ability to contribute both offensively and defensively at a high level. His bat-to-ball skills and projectable power give him long-term middle-of-the-order potential. Early All-America recognition suggests his ceiling is not just high—it’s already materializing.

Sam Cozart brings an imposing physical presence to the mound, and his raw tools suggest frontline potential for Texas. Standing 6-foot-6 with a power arm, he fits the archetype of a high-upside collegiate starter capable of overpowering hitters. As a freshman, his development hinges on command refinement, but the foundational traits—velocity, downhill plane, and durability—are already evident. Within a loaded Texas pitching staff, Cozart represents a long-term difference-maker.

Cam Appenzeller entered college baseball as one of the most highly regarded arms in his class, and expectations remain substantial. A hard-throwing left-hander with advanced feel for pitching, he combines velocity with a developing secondary arsenal. His ability to miss bats and handle high-leverage situations makes him a valuable asset even early in his career. Appenzeller’s trajectory points toward becoming a cornerstone arm in Tennessee’s rotation.

Rett Johnson profiles as a power arm with the type of raw stuff that translates well at the collegiate level. His fastball shows life through the zone, and his developing breaking pitches give him multiple avenues for generating swings and misses. As with many freshman pitchers, consistency will determine his immediate impact, but the ceiling is undeniable. Johnson’s progression will be closely monitored as he adjusts to higher-level competition.

Michael Senay is a versatile athlete whose value lies in both his defensive flexibility and offensive upside. Ranked among notable freshmen in his conference, he brings a dynamic skill set that allows him to contribute in multiple roles. His quick hands and athleticism suggest he can grow into a reliable offensive contributor as he gains experience. Senay’s adaptability should keep him on the field and accelerating his development curve.

Nicholas Partida entered the college ranks as a highly regarded recruit with strong offensive instincts. His advanced approach at the plate and ability to control the strike zone make him a difficult out, even against experienced pitching. Defensively, he offers stability and versatility, further increasing his value to Texas A&M. Partida’s well-rounded game gives him one of the higher floors among this freshman class.

Jett Kenady stands out for his defensive acumen behind the plate, a critical trait for any freshman catcher adjusting to the collegiate level. His receiving skills, arm strength, and game management ability allow him to earn early trust from pitching staffs. Offensively, he shows flashes of gap power with room to develop into a more consistent contributor. Kenady’s defensive foundation gives him a clear path to long-term impact.

Caden Glauber brings a polished repertoire to the mound, highlighted by his ability to mix pitches and disrupt timing. Unlike many freshmen arms, he relies less on raw velocity and more on command and pitch sequencing. That approach often translates well early in college careers, allowing him to compete immediately. As his physical tools continue to develop, Glauber could evolve into a highly dependable rotation piece.

Sawyer Pruitt offers intriguing two-way potential, combining offensive upside with defensive versatility. His swing is geared for consistent contact, but there is emerging power that could make him more than just a table-setter. On defense, his athleticism allows him to handle multiple positions effectively. Players with this kind of flexibility often become invaluable contributors over a long season.

Cole Hansen is a developmental arm with the kind of projection that coaches covet at the collegiate level. His frame and mechanics suggest there is still velocity to unlock, and his pitch mix provides a foundation for growth. Early in his career, his role may fluctuate, but his long-term outlook remains promising. Hansen represents the type of freshman who can make significant strides with structured development.

The 2026 freshman class represents a compelling mix of immediate production and long-term upside, with several players already performing like seasoned contributors. Their early impact underscores the evolving pipeline of talent entering college baseball, where readiness is no longer reserved for upperclassmen. As the season progresses, this group has the potential to shape both the national landscape and the future of the sport.

title: Huff scores career-high 38 as West Virginia tops Oklahoma 89-82 in OT to win CBC title
description: Honor Huff made eight 3-pointers and scored a career-high 38 to propel West Virginia to an 89-82 overtime victory over Oklahoma on Sunday in the championship game of the College Basketball Crown Tournament.  The senior guard teamed up with Jasper Floyd to do all the scoring in a game-ending 13-0 run for the Mountaineers (21-14) after spotting the Sooners (21-16) the first six points in OT.  Floyd missed his first eight shots before sinking 3-pointers on both sides of one by Huff in a 9-0 run that gave West Virginia the lead for good at 85-82.
fullText:
LAS VEGAS (AP) — Honor Huff made eight 3-pointers and scored a career-high 38 to propel West Virginia to an 89-82 overtime victory over Oklahoma on Sunday in the championship game of the College Basketball Crown Tournament.

The senior guard teamed up with Jasper Floyd to do all the scoring in a game-ending 13-0 run for the Mountaineers (21-14) after spotting the Sooners (21-16) the first six points in OT. Floyd missed his first eight shots before sinking 3-pointers on both sides of one by Huff in a 9-0 run that gave West Virginia the lead for good at 85-82. Huff scored the final four points at the foul line.

Huff made 9 of 19 shots with seven of the misses coming from beyond the arc. He made all 12 of his free throws and had four assists. Chance Moore totaled 19 points — on 7-for-8 shooting — and 10 rebounds off the bench. Brenen Lorient added 15 points and eight rebounds.

Nijel Pack led Oklahoma with 24 points and seven assists. Tae Davis had 19 points and Xzayvier Brown scored 13.

Huff hit his first five 3-pointers and West Virginia jumped out to a 26-11 lead in the first 9:25. Davis had a three-point play in a 9-0 run and another one with 22 seconds left as Oklahoma battled back to lead 41-37 at halftime.

Pack sank a 3-pointer 3:31 into the second half to give Oklahoma its first double-digit lead 52-42.

Huff hit a 3 and two free throws and Lorient scored after a Sooners' shot-clock violation to tie it 71-all with 1:45 remaining.

Pack and Huff traded 3-pointers, but Davis turned it over and then fouled Huff, who hit both free throws with 33 seconds remaining. Davis tied it at 76 with a layup, forcing OT.

title: West Virginia wears the Crown well
description: The Mountaineers, led by senior guard Honor Huff's 38 points, defeated Oklahoma in overtime, 89-82, to capture the College Basketball Crown event Sunday.
fullText:
Honor Huff, who stands 5-foot-10 and grew up playing basketball at Tillary Park in Brooklyn, where reputations are earned amid hard-fought battles, showed how good and tough he was, scoring 38 points and leading West Virginia to the College Basketball Crown title with an 89-82 overtime win over Oklahoma.

“It was a game with lots of ebbs and flows,” West Virginia coach Ross Hodge said. “We knew they were going to answer but this group has shown the ability to make big plays and get stops.”

The Mountaineers, who finished 21-14, earned $300,000 for their NIL collective. You can bet the money will be put to good use by Ross Hodge as the transfer portal opens Tuesday and it will be a frenetic 14 days as thousands of players seek new environs while chasing after a big payday.

Let’s not forget that while the Crown is a basketball tournament, it’s also a business proposition. Despite losing Sunday, Oklahoma received $100,000. The money matters.

But winning also matters. Only three teams will be able to say they were champions — West Virginia with the Crown, the winner of the NIT final between Tulsa and Auburn and, of course, Monday’s NCAA championship game between Connecticut and Michigan.

It gives Hodge and his staff momentum as it prepares to reconfigure the West Virginia roster as seven seniors will be departing.

“Nothing bad comes from winning,” Hodge said. “You get to win the last game of the year, it’s special.”  
Think the portal isn’t important? West Virginia won Sunday with a roster that included two players from North Texas, one from Chattanooga, one from St. Bonaventure, a player from Troy, a guy from Weber State by way of Japan, a transfer from South Carolina via Finland, one from North Dakota and one from UNC Wilmington. That what happens these days. You re-tool your roster and hope it comes together.

For a while, it looked as though Huff was going to singlehandedly carry West Virginia to the title. The guard in the turquoise sneakers who grew up in Brooklyn’s Bedford-Stuyvesant neighborhood and later Canarsie before going to prep school in Pennsylvania and first Virginia Military Institute before Chattanooga made his first five 3-point attempts as the Mountaineers opened up a 26-11 lead. That wasn’t surprising given Huff had led the nation with 131 treys while playing at Chattanooga a year ago before transferring to Morgantown.

But Huff cooled off as did his teammates. Suddenly Oklahoma was chipping away and turned the tables on West Virginia, using a 24-4 run to take a 36-30 lead with 2:21 left in the first half. The Sooners were still in front at the halftime buzzer, 41-37.

What happened? Oklahoma (21-16) has six seniors on its roster. It doesn’t rattle easy. And what was ironic was West Virginia’s roster has seven seniors, yet the Mountaineers found themselves struggling late in the half. No doubt Porter Moser reminded his guys that it’s a long game and as long as they didn’t lose contact with West Virginia, they were still in it.

“I think both teams battled their tails off,” Moser said. “There were a lot of guys who didn’t want their career to end.”

Oklahoma extended the advantage to 54-42 four-plus minutes into the second half. And while West Virginia made a run to tie things at 71 with 1:45 remaining, Huff got hot again and he hit from outside and from the foul line as West Virginia forced OT at 76-76. The Mountaineers with their seven seniors on their roster showed some poise themselves in coming back.

Huff had 31 points in 39 minutes of regulation. He added a corner 3 that tied it 82-82 with 2:14 left in OT, then drained a couple of free throws late as the Mountaineers finished what they started Sunday.

“Some of the shots Huff hit, you’ve got to tip your hat to him,” Moser said.

Huff said he never stopped believing that the next shot he took would drop.

“It’s not me,” he said. ”It’s these guys telling me to shoot it. They have the utmost confidence in me.”

It was a wild 45 minutes and for FOX Sports, which was looking to generate some late-season college hoops programming, it got what it was hoping for. The Crown downsized itself from 16 teams in 2025 to just eight this year, but it worked out fine.

Just ask West Virginia.

title: Has UCLA ever won a championship in women's basketball? History of Bruins' Final Four appearances
description: UCLA is one of the most consequential programs in women's college basketball history. Its tournament exploits are finally starting to reflect that stature.
fullText:
Has UCLA ever won a championship in women's basketball? History of Bruins' Final Four appearances originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

After years of waiting, UCLA women's basketball is finally starting to reach its potential.

The Bruins have emerged as one of women's college basketball's finest teams in recent years, benefitting from the strong play of standouts Lauren Betts and Kiki Rice.

UCLA is one of the most consequential programs in women's basketball history. The Bruins were the first team to offer a four-year athletic scholarship to a woman athlete and have had served as home to some of the sport's greatest luminaries. Their tournament showings are starting to reinforce their status as women's college basketball heavyweights.

Here's a look at UCLA's women's basketball championship history.

MORE: Projecting where Kiki Rice, Lauren Betts, other college stars will be picked in 2026 WNBA Draft

UCLA has never won a national championship -- at least not at the NCAA level. The Bruins did claim an AIAW tournament crown in 1978, hoisting the title after besting Maryland, 90-74 in the final game.

Ann Meyers, Denise Curry and Anita Ortega spearheaded a gifted UCLA bunch, which posted a 27-3 record and perfect 8-0 record in the WCAA. Meyers exploded in the national title game, recording 20 points, 10 rebounds, nine assists and eight steals. Ortega tacked on a game-high 23 points as Billie Moore's team hoisted its first season-ending tournament crown.

The Bruins joined the NCAA ahead of the 1981-82 season. Since then, they've made it to the Final Four on two occasions: 2025 and 2026.

MORE: What did Geno Auriemma say to Dawn Staley?

Before 2026, the last time UCLA earned a spot in the Final Four was...2025. It was a season filled with firsts for Cori Close's team, which ascended to the No. 1 spot in the AP rankings for the first time after besting South Carolina in Nov. 2024. The hits came on coming for the Bruins, who beat rival USC in the Big Ten title game before landing a No. 1 seed and No. 1 overall seed in the NCAA tournament.

UCLA secured commanding wins against Southern, Richmond and Ole Miss before slipping beyond No. 3 LSU to advance to the Final Four. Its dreams of further riches vanished as UConn raced to a 34-point win in the Final Four.

MORE: Iowa State sensation Audi Crooks enters transfer portal

With their appearance in the 2026 national championship game, the Bruins reached a new high-water mark in their program history. Prior to their 2026 flourish, UCLA's best finish in an NCAA tournament was that aforementioned Final Four.

Sarah Strong, Paige Bueckers and Azzi Fudd proved too powerful for the Bruins on that day, combining for 57 points -- six more than the Bruins scored as a whole. The result would serve as something of a premonition for the Huskies, who would capture their 12th national championship two days later.

title: College grads in ‘AI-proof’ careers like psychology and education are seeing negative returns on their degrees
description: As AI reshapes white-collar work, new research shows that some of the most popular graduate degrees are actually leaving holders worse off financially.
fullText:
There’s a boom in the economy: economics papers on the souring prospects of the recent college graduate in the AI-era economy of the 2020s. Harvard economists Lawrence Katz and Claudia Goldin found in September 2025 that the college wage premium remains, but has barely moved since 2000, while the San Francisco Fed attributed that stagnation primarily to less demand for those workers, in a working paper shortly afterward. The World Economic Forum found earlier this year that AI skills now command a 23% wage premium versus only 8% for a bachelor’s degree in isolation. Dallas Fed economist J. Scott Davis may have made the biggest splash in February 2026 with a paper that found AI is simultaneously reducing entry-level hiring and raising wages for experienced workers in the same AI-exposed occupations.

Why are engineering degrees showing surprisingly low returns?

Which graduate degrees offer negative financial returns?

How are AI skills changing traditional college wage premiums?

How does AI threaten white-collar job security prospects?

But what about the college grads that intentionally got degrees in supposedly “AI-proof” disciplines, like psychology or education?

A new report released by the Postsecondary Education and Economic Research Center maps out the estimated payoff of a graduate degree. When factoring in the costs of a graduate degree—tuition and fees—some degree holders are actually coming out the other end with negative returns. The worst returns are for psychology graduate degrees, with a -8% cost-adjusted return, or the estimated change in lifetime income after accounting for the cost of attendance.

The report also found that clinical psychology—a specialized branch of psychology—offers -5% cost-adjusted returns. Social work and curriculum and instruction degrees also offer negative returns, according to the study. Other popular degrees, such as computer science, yield only a 6% return after adjusting for costs.

“If you’re thinking about graduate school, you want to get some information about what the earnings potential is coming out of the degree as well as the kinds of occupations and jobs it leads to,” Joseph G. Altonji, a professor of economics at Yale and co-author of the study, told Fortune.

Over the years, more and more students have hedged their bets on a graduate degree to boost their salaries. The percentage of Americans with a graduate degree grew from 31% in 1993 to 42% in 2022, according to the U.S. Census Bureau. But as AI threatens the future of white-collar work, Gen Z, the generation just entering the workforce, is being forced to break with traditional work norms as the technology sparks a white-collar reckoning.

Research from Anthropic last month revealed that AI is theoretically capable of performing the majority of tasks in white-collar fields, such as engineering, law, and business and finance. As the Census suggests, many are still turning to the post-graduate degree (but a growing number are also ditching college altogether). Yet even as AI threatens to take jobs, some of the roles considered relatively safe from automation offer little in the way of job security.

title: Brayden Burries’s Parents Are Both Former College Athletes
description: Here's what to know about basketball star Brayden Burries and his parents, Hannah and Bobby, who both played college sports.
fullText:
"Hearst Magazines and Yahoo may earn commission or revenue on some items through these links."

Brayden Burries has only been playing college basketball for a year and he’s already one of the biggest stars in the NCAA Men’s Final Four this year. Brayden grew up in San Bernardino, California, attending Riverside Polytechnic High School before transferring to Eleanor Roosevelt High School after his freshman year. As a senior, he won the California CIF State Basketball Championship with Roosevelt and was selected to play in the 2025 McDonald's All-American Boys Game. After some wooing from multiple college programs, he now plays for the Arizona Wildcats of the Big 12 Conference.

The 2026 NBA draft is expected to take place in late June. In a recent mock draft from USA Today, Brayden is already considered so talented he could go in the first round. (That doesn’t mean he has confirmed or announced anything yet.) “Burries has also looked like a lottery talent during March Madness, earning a spot in the Sweet 16,” wrote Bryan Kalbrosky, USA Today’s senior NBA staff writer. “He has proven productivity and he is able to defend, relocate, move the ball and make 3-pointers off the dribble.”

Brayden comes from a sports household, and both of his parents are often seen at his Arizona games. Below, get to know Brayden’s parents, Hannah and Bobby Burries.

Brayden’s mother, Hannah, played two seasons of collegiate softball at Tennessee and one season of basketball at Cal State San Bernardino as a graduate transfer, earning All-Conference honors and a degree in business administration. It was at Cal State San Bernardino that she met her husband, Bobby, who played on the men’s basketball team. They have been together 21 years and have five children, all with “B” names: BJ, Brianna, Brayden, Brealani, and Brielle.

After college, Hannah was a teacher for six years before joining United Airlines, where she has worked for nearly two decades. She was awarded the United 100 award in 2017, given annually to only 100 employees companywide for exceptional performance, and received the honor again in 2023.

Bobby was also a star athlete in school. He started out playing both football and basketball in high school before focusing on college basketball at San Bernardino Valley College and Cal State San Bernardino, where he is now a member of the latter’s Hall of Fame. He went on to play professionally overseas. Brayden has credited his father with instilling a winning mentality in him from an early age. “That’s helped me get to the point where I’m at today,” Brayden said in 2025.

title: President Donald Trump's executive order sells 1 thing to college sports in NCAA president's eyes
description: NCAA President Charlie Baker spoke before Saturday's Final Four games about the executive order signed by Donald Trump.
fullText:
INDIANAPOLIS — NCAA President Charlie Baker is under no illusions that a recent move from the White House will solve every one of the myriad problems facing his membership.

Baker is hopeful, though, that the executive order signed by President Trump on Friday can act as what it was perhaps intended to be from the beginning: A firm hand in the back of Congress to find a reasonable solution to those problems through legislative action.

If the order does that, it will have managed what nothing else has, in beginning to ease the turmoil plaguing college sports through the last half-decade. Which makes that outcome possible, but difficult to imagine.

“I think part of the message from him,” Baker, referring to President Trump, said, “is, can we find a way to push this a little harder through the legislative process, and get something on the books that represents what most people are looking for at this point? Which is a much simpler eligibility process.”

Eligibility sat at the center of the executive order signed Friday. It sets a range of parameters on that issue — from barring professionals from returning to college, to setting athletes’ eligibility window at five years, to limiting those athletes to one penalty free transfer in their careers.

There are other concerns discussed in a directive many, including those within the executive branch itself, expect to be challenged legally. The order directs the NCAA, per a report from Yahoo! Sports, to “update its rules” accordingly by Aug. 1, which at least allows runway for any potential changes and does not immediately affect college sports’ day-to-day operation.

Baker acknowledged the difficulties associated with some aspects of the order. Not least the directive to bring the association’s rules in line, understandable in theory but difficult in practice when the NCAA is already facing varied challenges to its eligibility rules in the courts.

Still, the NCAA’s fourth-year leader expressed optimism at the potential for President Trump’s order to shake loose enough congressional action to make meaningful progress toward national legislation.

“There are a number of Democrats who said what I would describe as some reasonably positive things about some key elements of it,” Baker said, adding with a smile, “not a lot of people say nice things about much of anything” at the legislative level right now.

What Baker will want it to help specifically is the SCORE Act, legislation introduced in July and still working its way through the House.

Widely supported by NCAA membership, the SCORE Act would carve out a limited antitrust exemption for the association while creating federal standards around issues including name, image and likeness. Both the Big Ten Conference and Indiana University individually endorsed Friday’s executive order as a means of driving the legislative process forward around the SCORE Act specifically.

It has drawn criticism from both sides of the political spectrum, including concerns over everything from athletes’ rights, long-term financial concerns, congressional overreach and more.

Struggling for the required votes in the House, the SCORE Act would almost certainly face an even steeper road in the Senate, where legislators have introduced bipartisan bills on the issue as well.

Whether the President’s executive order can trigger meaningful progress in a process currently struggling for it remains to be seen. Baker’s hopeful comments might not amount to much more than that — hope.

For his membership, it at least represents fresh support for movement in a stalled process, as the NCAA continues to search for better legal and regulatory footing. If it does shake the trees to the point that something finally falls out, it will have accomplished progress nothing and no one else has to this point. Which is another way of saying: Believe it when you see it.

Zach Osterman is IndyStar's IU insider. Listen to Mind Your Banners, our IU Athletics-centric podcast, on Apple Podcasts, Spotify or wherever you get your podcasts. Watch the latest on IndyStar TV: Hoosiers.

This article originally appeared on Indianapolis Star: Trump executive order signals hope for NCAA president Charlie Baker

title: Crews battle fast-growing wildfire in windy Southern California that's forced some to evacuate
description: Crews battled a smoky and fast-growing wildfire Friday in windy Southern California that forced some residents to evacuate and a community college to temporarily close its doors.  The Springs Fire bro...
fullText:
RIVERSIDE, Calif. (AP) — Crews battled a smoky and fast-growing wildfire Friday in windy Southern California that forced some residents to evacuate and a community college to temporarily close its doors.

The Springs Fire broke out around 11 a.m. Friday and by the evening had grown to about 6.5 square miles (16.8 square kilometers). The cause of the fire east of Moreno Valley in Riverside County is under investigation. It was not immediately known how many households were under evacuation warnings or orders.

With hundreds of people battling the blaze — using helicopters, engines and water tenders — crews started to contain the blaze by Friday night.

The fire was burning in a populated — but not densely so — unincorporated part of Riverside County in a recreational area near the city of Moreno Valley, which has a population of roughly 200,000. The city is 10 miles (16 kilometers) southeast of Riverside and 64 miles (103 kilometers) east of Los Angeles.

“It’s windy out there,” said Maggie Cline De La Rosa, a public information officer for the California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection in Riverside County.

Alex Izaguirre, a spokesperson for the Cal Fire Riverside County, said the wind is “spreading the smoke,” prompting concerned calls from residents in neighboring cities who can see and smell it.

Moreno Valley College's main campus was shuttered Friday and Saturday due to the fire impacting the air quality.

“All students, faculty, and staff are being directed to leave campus immediately,” the school said in a post on Instagram.

California U.S. Rep. Raul Ruiz said in a social media post that he was closely monitoring the blaze.

“If you are under an evacuation order, please leave immediately,” he said.

The National Weather Service issued a wind advisory for San Bernardino and Riverside County valleys through Saturday afternoon, with gusts of up to 50 mph (80 kph) expected.

“Tree limbs could be blown down and a few power outages may result,” the advisory read.

title: Final Four 2026: Low-scoring semifinals were 'not pretty in any way, shape or form.' Will NCAA championship be any different?
description: Oh, you thought the four best teams in women’s college basketball over the past two seasons would show up at the Final Four and put on an offensive clinic? That’s hilarious.
fullText:
PHOENIX — Oh, you thought the four best teams in women’s college basketball over the past two seasons would show up at the Final Four and put on an offensive clinic?

The consequence of a sport that prides itself on defensive buy-in is that matchups of the elite come down to which team can crack 50.

Whether fans can stomach it is less concrete a mark than those involved recognize.

“I wanted to apologize to all the fans for the rugby match and the 23 turnovers,” was the first thing UCLA head coach Cori Close said in the post-game press conference. Asked later if she thought it would be that much of a defensive battle, she scoffed her denial.

UCLA advanced to its first NCAA national championship game with a 51-44 victory over Texas that barely crossed the scoring threshold. South Carolina moved forward in a 62-48 win over UConn that didn’t develop an offensive attitude until the second half.

UCLA defeats Texas to advance to its first-ever NCAA title game!

🎥 ESPN pic.twitter.com/IOYVnedeRO

— NCAA March Madness (@MarchMadnessWBB) April 4, 2026

There were more bodies on the floor than made field goals, an embellishment that might actually be true for the UCLA-Texas nightcap in which they combined for 35 turnovers, 10 more than the first game.

The ugly showing lodged itself into various record slots. And not the fun kind.

The six points UCLA held Texas to in the first quarter are tied for the fewest in any quarter in a national semifinal game, and the second-fewest in a quarter of a Final Four game. The 37 combined points in the first half set the record for fewest points in a half, and the second-fewest of any half of a Final Four game. The 95 combined points is the third-lowest combined score in women’s Final Four history, narrowly beating out the 93-point total in 2008 between Tennessee and LSU.

It must haunt Texas head coach Vic Schaefer that he said so many times at the Fort Worth regional his team didn’t have to “live and die with every defensive possession to try and grind out a 61-59 win.”

Because that’s exactly what UCLA made them do. But somehow, worse. What he said will haunt him “probably till the day I die,” is that his locker room felt like “we let one get away.”

Their offensive output was a major point of pride for Schaefer, a defensive hoop head, heading into the weekend. They even had late-game opportunities to hit that one shot, the type that ousted him from a national championship at the last second. And they didn’t go.

“I do not know why the good lord picked tonight for us not to be able to make a shot,” Schaefer said in a highly emotional post-game press conference. “That’s the hand we got dealt, so … we’ll move on.”

The NCAA tournament’s most dominant team cleared a season-low scoring total in the wrong direction by 20 points. It’s not that his squad, led by exiting fifth-year redshirt senior Rori Harmon, didn’t put up its own standout effort.

UCLA’s total was the lowest of its season, dropping below the previous bottom-barrel 65 in a loss to Texas in November. The Longhorns scored 76 in that 11-point win, quite nearly double their total on Friday.

“We were having trouble earning quality shots,” Close said.

UConn and South Carolina were better, but barely. The night’s glaring issue was the lack of individual firepower from the game’s brightest backcourt stars. There were few major buckets that felt like they would shift the momentum, or at minimum make a highlight reel. They certainly won’t be names etched into the annals of this year’s tournament, at least not tonight.

UConn’s Sarah Strong, the National Player of the Year winner, and Azzi Fudd, a projected WNBA lottery pick, combined to shoot 7 of 31. Fudd went 2 of 9 from the perimeter, a struggle for the Huskies until the third quarter.

South Carolina’s duo of Ta’Niya Latson and Raven Johnson went 4 of 16, though Latson hit all 10 free throws. And in a brutal finale, Texas sophomore Madison Booker was 3 of 23. Combined, the five were 20% from the field.

“I want to grow the game so bad, I felt guilty walking off the floor because it was not pretty in any way, shape or form,” Close said.

Don’t expect much more from the national title game. South Carolina is known for its defense and ability to muck up a game the way SEC talents deliver. UCLA showed it could also play a smothering style.

Will anyone be “giving their flowers” to these offenses? Absolutely not.

Does it matter if it puts you into the national championship game? UCLA and South Carolina certainly don’t think so.

title: President Trump delivers new executive order attempting to regulate college sports
description: The order intends to limit athlete transfer movement, cap player eligibility, require funding requirements for women and Olympic sports, and regulate NIL collectives.
fullText:
INDIANAPOLIS, Ind. — On the eve of the tip-off to the Final Four, the president of the United States is making a splash.

President Donald Trump issued his latest executive order to regulate college sports on Friday.

The 10-page order comes a day before the NCAA’s crown jewel — the men’s basketball tournament — reaches its pinnacle event here in central Indiana.

The order grants the NCAA the ability and suggests the organization limit athlete transfer movement, cap player eligibility, implement funding requirements for women and Olympic sports, and prohibit NIL collectives. As an enforcement lever, the order relies on the reduction of a university’s federal funding — an incentive for schools and conferences to abide by the concepts.

The order directs the NCAA to update its rules by Aug. 1 — to the maximum extent permitted by law — to “bring order and stability to the landscape in certain key areas,” a source who has reviewed the document told Yahoo Sports. Most notably, one of those areas is transferring.

Compliance with these rules will be relevant in determining if schools will continue to receive federal funding.

Many — including the president himself — expect the order to be challenged legally.

Perhaps the most significant concepts in the document are efforts to regulate athlete movement and compensation.

The order not only grants but commands the association to create strict guardrails around booster-backed NIL collectives — what it describes as “fraudulent NIL schemes” — and limit the movement of transfers by reinstating the NCAA’s “one-time” transfer rule. The courts deemed that rule unlawful through antitrust rulings. The rule would permit athletes to transfer once before requiring them to miss one season as a penalty for any subsequent moves.

The order does not unilaterally and immediately change the transfer rule, a critical concept. The language is key as thousands of players — some of whom have already transferred once — are preparing to enter the basketball portal, which opens Tuesday.

The order prohibits professional athletes from returning to play in college and encourages the NCAA to define an athlete’s eligibility window at five years. The NCAA eligibility standard is currently four competitive seasons over five years. This is a critical topic that even the most ardent NCAA detractors believe should be regulated.

In the last year, more than 70 athletes have filed suit against the governing body, as players use state and local judges to grant them an extension of their eligibility. The NCAA has spent $16 million alone litigating eligibility cases.

Trump writes in the order that the NCAA should implement revenue-share that "preserves or expands scholarships" in women's and Olympic sports; prohibit federal funds to be used for NIL or rev-share; and prohibit "improper financial activities ... including collectives."

Women’s and Olympic sports are said to be a focus for Trump, who believes that non-revenue programs are being eliminated or at least defunded, as schools shift more resources to football and men’s basketball in an intense and competitive recruiting environment where athlete compensation has been legalized.

Lastly, the order invalidates certain state laws that conflict with the order, which are likely to include several state statutes governing NIL.

But the order’s true impact remains unclear and is in doubt considering that Trump’s previous executive order, released in July, has created no real results within the industry. This one, however, is more comprehensive and direct as opposed to the last one, which only directed his cabinet members to create rules — which never materialized.

Executive orders are subject to legal scrutiny, especially those that disregard court orders. In fact, courts have struck down several of the president’s orders over the last several months, rendering them moot and unenforceable. In a White House roundtable event last month, the president himself predicted that any order would be legally challenged. He said that he “hoped” for a favorable judge.

Why can’t the industry “go back to the old system?” Trump asked a room of dignitaries at the March 6 roundtable event. “I’d like to go exactly back to what we had and ram it through a court.”

Like the roundtable event itself, the order is likely geared toward bringing attention to the issue in effort to pressure Congressional lawmakers to reach an agreement on a more concrete solution: legislation. That’s something of which lawmakers have failed to do in seven years of lobbying from the NCAA for a bill to, most notably, permit college sports leaders to enact and enforce rules without them being legally challenged — in other words, an antitrust exemption.

However, divide rages among those on either side of the aisle over an issue that many originally thought to be bipartisan in nature. That hasn’t proven to be true.

Republicans support a more narrow NCAA-leaning bill with athlete restrictions; Democrats, many of them harsh critics of the NCAA and power conference leadership, are supporting a more broad bill with athlete freedoms.

Despite confidence from Republican leadership in the House, the Republican-authored SCORE Act — the one piece of all-encompassing legislation to emerge from a committee — has twice failed to reach the House floor for a vote. Lawmakers are working to bring SCORE to the floor by the month’s end, but they continue the process of whipping votes. Holding a slim House majority, Republicans cannot afford to lose their own members, some of whom oppose portions of the bill.

Even if it advances out of the House, SCORE needs significant modifications to pass a U.S. Senate that requires a 60-vote margin for approval of legislation. That means seven Democrats voting in favor of the measure — a tall task.

In the Senate, Sens. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Maria Cantwell (D-Wash.) are holding separate negotiations over a bill, though if past discussions are any indication, the two disagree on a wide variety of bill concepts, most notably government oversight of college sports, athlete employment and the breadth of antitrust protections.

Meanwhile, five presidential committees — made up of college sports stakeholders, business executives and other dignitaries — began meeting this week with the goal of informing congressional legislation. Each committee is charged with studying an issue, plus a sixth group, an oversight committee, to review their work.

The oversight committee includes six presidents/chancellors from Georgia, Nebraska, Tennessee, Kansas, Utah and North Carolina, plus former Clemson president Jim Clements, Cody Campbell, Randy Levine and Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis. The five “issues” committees are Legislative (work with Congress for federal antitrust protection), Rules (determine NIL, portal, eligibility standards), NCAA Reform (future governance), Media (media rights and SBA), and Player-Agent relationship issues.

Commissioners from the SEC, ACC, Big 12, Big Ten and American as well as Notre Dame AD Pete Bevacqua are assigned to Rules, Media and NCAA Reform committees as well as many other notable names, including Nick Saban, Condoleezza Rice and Adam Silver.

Here’s what each power conference commissioner had to say after the release of the executive order on Friday night.

title: Sacramento veteran prepares for Boston Marathon while fundraising for USO after overcoming medical setback
description: A Sacramento resident is preparing to run the Boston Marathon as part of a charity effort supporting military service members and their families.  Brian Kropf, an Army veteran and EMT, is training for the race with Team USO while attending the American River College Fire Academy.  Kropf said his schedule has shifted significantly in recent months as he balances training with coursework and physical demands tied to the fire academy.
fullText:
A Sacramento resident is preparing to run the Boston Marathon as part of a charity effort supporting military service members and their families.

Brian Kropf, an Army veteran and EMT, is training for the race with Team USO while attending the American River College Fire Academy. His campaign includes fundraising for the United Service Organizations.

Kropf said his schedule has shifted significantly in recent months as he balances training with coursework and physical demands tied to the fire academy. “My life had gotten a lot more complicated. I joined a fire academy… it’s keeping me super busy and super physical and mental,” he said.

His preparation was also interrupted by a medical issue during a key training period. Kropf was hospitalized for several days with bacterial pneumonia and sepsis, leading to a pause in his running schedule. He has since resumed training, working to rebuild endurance ahead of race day.

“It’s been slow. Everything is just harder. I’m really just clawing back every mile I can to prepare me as well as I can be for Boston,” Kropf said.

Kropf served in the U.S. Army from 2006 to 2012, including time with the 82nd Airborne Division and deployments to Iraq and Haiti. After his military service, he attended the University of California, Berkeley, later transitioning into the private sector before pursuing a career in emergency services.

He said his decision to run is connected to his military experience and his familiarity with the USO, an organization that provides programs and services for service members and their families. His fundraising is part of a broader effort by Team USO runners participating in the marathon.

Kropf is continuing to train in Sacramento while preparing for certification as a firefighter. He said the goal now is to reach the starting line and complete the race while representing those who have supported his effort.

The Boston Marathon is scheduled for later this month, with participants from across the country raising funds for affiliated nonprofit organizations.

Here is a link to Brian's fundraising efforts ahead of the race: Brian Runs Boston to Support Those Who Serve

title: Renegades win top WSC tennis awards; BC swimmers host meet at Garces; High schools return; CSUB roundup
description: The Bakersfield College men’s and women’s tennis teams have competed in the Western State Conference Singles and Doubles tournament since Tuesday and will do so through Saturday at Ventura College. On Thursday, the WSC announced all-conference selections and individual awards, too. The BC men’s team, which captured the conference title last week, earned numerous all-WSC honors, including two ...
fullText:
The Bakersfield College men’s and women’s tennis teams have competed in the Western State Conference Singles and Doubles tournament since Tuesday and will do so through Saturday at Ventura College.

On Thursday, the WSC announced all-conference selections and individual awards, too. The BC men’s team, which captured the conference title last week, earned numerous all-WSC honors, including two of the top individual awards.

Mateusz Lipczynski was named the WSC Men’s Player of the Year. A freshman from Warsaw, Poland, Lipczynski has been the top singles player and a solid doubles player for this Renegades conference championship team. He came into the WSC Singles and Doubles Tournament as the No. 1 seed in singles play. He went into Friday’s tournament action playing in the semifinal round of singles and in doubles with his partner, Orlando Vankan.

Noel Dalton, who has been BC tennis coach since 2018, was named WSC Men’s Coach of the Year. Dalton played tennis at BC in 1983 and 1984. He and volunteer assistant coach Hank Pfister Jr., a former pro tennis player and also a former Renegades player, directed BC to its first WSC team championship since BC joined the WSC in 1987.

Lipczynski and Dalton are the first BC recipients of these WSC honors since Adrian Bolton and then-head coach Rob Slaybaugh did it in the same year, 1996, following a second-place WSC team finish for the Renegades.

Here’s the complete list of all BC men’s players and their conference honors: Singles: First team, Lipczynski and Axl Lajon Gonzaga; Second team: Shang En Kao and Vitalie Chibac. Doubles: First team, Gonzaga/Kao, Lipczynski/Vankan; Second team, Zeb Duket/Chin Chang Wu.

For the WSC women’s all-conference selections, BC sophomores Naomi Pavletich and Kailani Lua were honorable mention singles selections and were honorable mention as a doubles team.

In the women’s WSC singles and doubles tournament, Lua, who had a first-round bye and then won her first two matches, lost on Friday afternoon to the No. 1 seed, College of the Canyons’ Emma Chodur. Lua was scheduled to play in a doubles match with Pavletich.

The WSC tournament action concludes Saturday.

The BC men’s and women’s swim teams will host their lone meet of the season on Saturday at a 10 a.m. tri-meet with Santa Maria’s Allan Hancock and Fresno City. The meet will be held at Garces High School, which has served as a temporary practice pool for the Renegades during season-long construction at their on-campus pool space.

“The move has definitely been a big adjustment, but Garces has been great to us,” BC Coach Matt Moon said.

It appears Moon has solid expectations for Saturday’s meet.

“We just came off a big week of training over spring break, so I’m hoping to see some clean swimming,” he said. “Everyone is in great shape mentally and physically going into this meet and excited to compete in Bakersfield.”

Saturday’s meet will be the final tune-up meet for the Western State Conference championships scheduled for April 16-18.

“We have a couple more weeks to fine-tune all the details of the swims and mentally lock in for three big days of racing at the WSC championships,” Moon said.

As spring break winds down for local high schools, and as spring sports teams trained, practiced, played in an out-of-town tournament or were able to rest a bit, early April is the time for league play to start up.

A few highlights this week should include a three-game baseball series between defending South Yosemite Valley League champion Bakersfield Christian — which traveled to Sacramento to play in the Boras Northern Classic — and Ridgeview, the defending Central Section Division 2 champions and CIF Division 4 Southern California (state) regional champions. The Wolf Pack played in a San Diego Lions Tournament last week. The Eagles and Wolf Pack will play Monday, Wednesday and Friday, each game starting at 4 p.m. BCHS hosts Monday and Friday games.

Another baseball series of note could be Frontier and Stockdale in the South Yosemite River League. These teams will play Tuesday, Wednesday and Friday at 4 p.m. Frontier hosts Tuesday and Friday.

There’s the annual Lonnie Shelton Track and Field Invitational that will be packed with local team entrants at 12:30 p.m. Friday at Foothill High School.

More distant is the April 17 boys and girls swim meet between Garces and Liberty at the McMurtrey Aquatics Center in downtown Bakersfield. Two of the top South Yosemite River League teams will swim starting at 3:30 p.m.

Baseball: The Roadrunners fell 14-4 in Thursday’s opening game of a three-game, non-conference series at home against Utah Valley. The Wolverines scored five runs in the third inning and combined for eight more runs in the seventh, eighth and ninth to take the first game. CSUB’s defense had four errors. The Roadrunners got three of their runs in the seventh inning on RBI hits by Jacob May and Elgin Bennett and a sacrifice fly by Devon Wilkes. The teams played again Friday night and will conclude the weekend with a 1 p.m. game Saturday at Hardt Field.

Softball: The Roadrunners played the Big West Conference Tournament opening game of a three-game series at home against Cal Poly on Friday. CSUB, which went into the game with a 6-27 overall mark and 4-8 in conference, had gone 2-2 in the opening game of four Big West series, including against UC Santa Barbara last week. The Roadrunners and Mustangs will play a doubleheader Saturday at noon and 2:30 p.m.

Beach volleyball: CSUB dropped its first two matches of this weekend’s Islanders Tournament, being hosted by Texas A&M Corpus Christi. The Roadrunners (7-18) battled but lost to tournament host A&M CC, 3-2. CSUB was then swept 5-0 by Pepperdine to complete Friday’s action. The team will play Louisiana Monroe and Tarleton State on Saturday morning.
